statement: Enforceability of terms-of-service (ToS) provisions when users do not click an explicit "I agree" turns principally on controlling primary authorities (statutes and binding case law) and the specific notice/assent facts, so source quality matters more than source volume.
rationale: Contract-formation doctrine (browsewrap vs. clickwrap), notice/conspicuity standards, and controlling precedents determine outcomes; therefore prioritize high-authority, on-point decisions over numerous low-value citations.

statement: Apparent contradictions among authorities will identify the critical follow-up questions needed to resolve enforceability—primarily whether notice was reasonably conspicuous, whether user conduct manifestly indicated assent, and which contractual form (browsewrap, clickwrap, sign-in wrap) was at issue.
rationale: Differences in holdings typically reflect distinct fact patterns or legal tests; mapping those distinctions pinpoints what additional factual or jurisdictional research is necessary.

statement: A decision-grade answer must state the legal standards, identify jurisdictional splits and limiting facts, and quantify uncertainty rather than offering just a binary conclusion about enforceability.
rationale: Practically useful research requires actionable limits (which facts matter, how strongly they influence outcomes, and where legal uncertainty remains) so clients can assess risk and fact-gathering priorities.
